---
title: "Claims Severity GLM Models Exploration"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    toc_depth: 3
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
  word_document: default
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Libraries Load

```{r, include=FALSE}
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(rsample)
library(recipes)
library(MASS)
library(survival)
library(pROC)
library(knitr)
library(corrplot)
library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)
library(patchwork)
```


# Data Exploration

The target variable avg_claim_cost is the average cost per claim for policies that recorded at least one claim in the observation year. The full distribution is highly right-skewed, with the vast majority of claims concentrated at low values and a long tail extending beyond €100,000. A zoomed-in view confirms the heavy mass below €1,000, with frequency dropping sharply thereafter. To operationalise this skew, a severity threshold of €900 is applied: claims at or below this value are classified as attritional and those above as extreme. At this cut-off, approximately 91% of claimants fall in the attritional segment, yielding roughly one extreme claim for every ten attritional claims.

```{r, echo=FALSE}

# =============================================================================
# A: DATA LOADING
# =============================================================================

basic_glm_data <- read.csv(
  here("Feature_Selection_Four_Wheels_GLM_R.csv")
)

basic_glm_data_no_na <- basic_glm_data[!is.na(basic_glm_data$avg_claim_cost), ]

h = ggplot(basic_glm_data_no_na, aes(avg_claim_cost)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 500, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Claims Severity Distribution",
    x = "Average Claim Cost",
    y = "Count"
  )

h

# 1. Save your plot to a variable
p = ggplot(basic_glm_data_no_na, aes(avg_claim_cost)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 200, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000)) +
  labs(
    title = "Claims Severity Distribution (Zoomed In)",
    x = "Average Claim Cost",
    y = "Count"
  )

p

# 2. Build the plot data
plot_data <- ggplot_build(p)$data[[1]]

benchmark <- plot_data$xmax[5]
pct_within <- mean(basic_glm_data_no_na$avg_claim_cost <= benchmark) * 100
total_claims <- nrow(basic_glm_data_no_na)
attritional_count <- sum(basic_glm_data_no_na$avg_claim_cost <= benchmark)
extreme_count <- total_claims - attritional_count
ratio_val <- round(extreme_count / attritional_count, 3)

# 2. Print the narrative to the output
cat(
  "At ", benchmark, ", ", round(pct_within, 2), "% of the data falls within. ",
  "This corresponds to ", attritional_count, " attritional claims and ",
  extreme_count, " extreme claims — meaning that for every 1 attritional claim, there are ",
  ratio_val, " extreme claims.",
  sep = ""
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# REVISED PLOT & ANALYSIS (Cutoff at 900)
# =============================================================================

# 1. Build the plot with the vertical line and text label
p = ggplot(basic_glm_data_no_na, aes(avg_claim_cost)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 200, fill = "blue", color = "white") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 5000)) +
  
  # Add the red dashed vertical line at 900
  geom_vline(xintercept = 900, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  
  # Add the floating text label near the top of the plot
  annotate(
    "text", 
    x = 1050,                  # Placed slightly to the right of the line so it doesn't overlap
    y = Inf,                   # Automatically pins it to the top of the y-axis
    label = "Cutoff: $900\n≤ Attritional (91.27%) | > Extreme (8.73%) ", 
    color = "red", 
    fontface = "bold",
    hjust = 0,                 # Left-aligns the text relative to the x coordinate
    vjust = 2                  # Nudges the text down slightly so it doesn't clip the top border
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Claims Severity Distribution (Zoomed In)",
    x = "Average Claim Cost",
    y = "Count"
  )

# Display the plot
p

# 2. Hardcode your new benchmark so your calculations match your visual
benchmark <- 900

pct_within <- mean(basic_glm_data_no_na$avg_claim_cost <= benchmark) * 100
total_claims <- nrow(basic_glm_data_no_na)
attritional_count <- sum(basic_glm_data_no_na$avg_claim_cost <= benchmark)
extreme_count <- total_claims - attritional_count
ratio_val <- round(extreme_count / attritional_count, 3)

# 3. Print the updated narrative
cat(
  "At $", benchmark, ", ", round(pct_within, 2), "% of the data falls within. ",
  "This corresponds to ", attritional_count, " attritional claims and ",
  extreme_count, " extreme claims — meaning that for every 1 attritional claim, there are ",
  ratio_val, " extreme claims.",
  sep = ""
)
```


# Data Pre-processing

The dataset is split at the client level (not the policy level) to prevent data leakage: all policies belonging to a given client are assigned exclusively to either the training or test set. Stratification is performed on a composite stratum combining whether the client ever lapsed, ever claimed, and the number of policies they hold, ensuring both splits are representative of the portfolio's behavioural structure. The final split produces 15,156 training policies and 3,788 test policies across 12,080 and 3,021 unique clients respectively. Within the training set, a 5-fold grouped cross-validation structure is constructed on the same client-level grouping, so that no client's data appears in both the analysis and assessment folds of any split. This CV structure is used throughout to produce stable, leak-free estimates of out-of-sample performance before evaluating on the hold-out test set.

Predictors are encoded inside each CV fold and on the full train/test split independently, so that dummy levels and zero-variance columns are learned only from training data and applied to validation/test data — preventing any look-ahead bias in the feature pipeline. Premium-related variables (premium_band, premium_vs_median_band) are excluded from all model matrices given their endogenous relationship with the target.


```{r, include=FALSE}

# =============================================================================
# C: VARIABLE DEFINITIONS
# =============================================================================

ordinal_levels <- list(
  age_category                     = c("18-25","26-35","36-45","46-55","56-65","65+"),
  age_when_licensed_band           = c("<=25",">25"),
  driving_exp_category             = c("<=5yrs","6-10yrs","11-20yrs","20+yrs"),
  vehicle_age_category             = c("0-3yrs","4-7yrs","8-12yrs","12+yrs"),
  power_band                       = c("<100",">100"),
  cylinder_band                    = c("0-1400","1400-1600","1600-2000","2000+"),
  value_band                       = c("0-17500","17500-25000","25000-32500","32500+"),
  weight_band                      = c("0-1100","1100-1500","1500+"),
  length_imputed_band              = c("<4","4-4.5",">4.5"),
  n_doors_band                     = c("<=4",">4"),
  claims_history_band              = c("zero","one","two_to_three","four_to_five","six_plus"),
  r_claims_history_band            = c("0","0-0.35","0.35-0.7","0.7-1.5","1.5+"),
  avg_claims_per_year_history_band = c("0","0-1",">1"),
  cross_sell_ratio_band            = c("<0.75",">=0.75"),
  policy_retention_rate_band       = c("<1","1"),
  risk_score_band                  = c("0","0-0.01","0.01-0.15","0.15-0.2","0.2+"),
  premium_band                     = c("0-250","250-300","300-350","350-400","400+"),
  premium_vs_median_band           = c("-15%","-15% to 0%","0% to 15%","15% to 30%","30%+")
)

always_exclude  <- c("id","id2","n_claims_year","avg_claim_cost",
                     "lapse_flag","days_to_event","exposure_years",
                     "r_claims_history_band","claims_history_band")
premium_exclude <- c("premium_band","premium_vs_median_band")
target          <- "avg_claim_cost"
THRESHOLD       <- 900          # extreme = cost > THRESHOLD


# =============================================================================
# D: CLIENT-STRATIFIED TRAIN–TEST SPLIT (80:20)
# =============================================================================

set.seed(42)
client_strat <- basic_glm_data_no_na %>%
  group_by(id) %>%
  summarise(ever_lapsed  = as.integer(any(lapse_flag == 1,   na.rm=TRUE)),
            ever_claimed = as.integer(any(n_claims_year > 0, na.rm=TRUE)),
            n_policies   = n(), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(policy_tier = case_when(n_policies==1~"1",n_policies==2~"2",TRUE~"3+"),
         strata       = paste(ever_lapsed, ever_claimed, policy_tier, sep="_"))

rare <- names(table(client_strat$strata)[table(client_strat$strata)==1])
client_strat <- client_strat %>%
  mutate(strata = if_else(strata %in% rare, "other", strata))

split_obj  <- initial_split(client_strat, prop=0.8, strata=strata)
train_data <- basic_glm_data_no_na %>% filter(id %in% training(split_obj)$id)
test_data  <- basic_glm_data_no_na %>% filter(id %in% testing(split_obj)$id)

stopifnot(length(intersect(unique(train_data$id), unique(test_data$id))) == 0)
cat(sprintf("Train: %d policies | %d clients\nTest : %d policies | %d clients\n",
            nrow(train_data), n_distinct(train_data$id),
            nrow(test_data),  n_distinct(test_data$id)))

# Claimant subsamples
train_sev <- train_data %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
test_sev  <- test_data  %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
cat(sprintf("Claimant rows — Train: %d  |  Test: %d\n", nrow(train_sev), nrow(test_sev)))


# =============================================================================
# E: GROUPED 5-FOLD CV STRUCTURE
# =============================================================================

set.seed(42)
cv_folds <- group_vfold_cv(train_data, group=id, v=5)
stopifnot(all(vapply(cv_folds$splits, function(sp)
  length(intersect(unique(analysis(sp)$id), unique(assessment(sp)$id))) == 0L,
  logical(1))))
cat("CV folds: 5 | Client-leakage-free: TRUE\n")


# =============================================================================
# F: ENCODING HELPERS
# =============================================================================

recode_ordinals <- function(df) {
  for (v in intersect(names(ordinal_levels), names(df)))
    df[[v]] <- factor(df[[v]], levels = ordinal_levels[[v]], ordered = FALSE)
  df
}

get_predictor_cols <- function(data, tgt)
  setdiff(names(data), unique(c(always_exclude, premium_exclude, tgt)))

encode_predictors <- function(train_df, test_df, tgt) {
  pcols   <- get_predictor_cols(train_df, tgt)
  x_tr    <- recode_ordinals(train_df[, pcols, drop=FALSE])
  x_te    <- recode_ordinals(test_df[,  pcols, drop=FALSE])
  rec     <- recipe(~., data=x_tr) %>%
    step_novel(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
    step_unknown(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%
    step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot=FALSE) %>%
    step_zv(all_predictors())
  prep_rec <- prep(rec, training=x_tr, retain=TRUE)
  list(train=bake(prep_rec, new_data=NULL),
       test =bake(prep_rec, new_data=x_te),
       recipe=prep_rec)
}

# Shorthand constructors for glm/lm to avoid column-name conflicts
fit_glm <- function(y, X, family) glm(resp__ ~ ., data=data.frame(resp__=y, X), family=family)
fit_lm  <- function(y, X)         lm(resp__  ~ ., data=data.frame(resp__=y, X))


# =============================================================================
# G: METRIC HELPERS
# =============================================================================

rmse          <- function(obs, pred)  sqrt(mean((obs - pred)^2, na.rm=TRUE))
mae           <- function(obs, pred)  mean(abs(obs - pred), na.rm=TRUE)
pseudo_r2_glm <- function(fit)        1 - fit$deviance / fit$null.deviance
r2_cor        <- function(obs, pred)  cor(obs, pred, use="complete.obs")^2
gamma_dev     <- function(obs, pred)  2 * sum(obs/pred - log(obs/pred) - 1, na.rm=TRUE)
ln_dev        <- function(obs, pred)  sum((log(obs) - log(pred))^2, na.rm=TRUE)
binom_dev     <- function(y_bin, p) {
  p <- pmax(pmin(p, 1-1e-10), 1e-10)
  -2 * sum(y_bin * log(p) + (1 - y_bin) * log(1 - p))
}
gini_index <- function(obs, pred) {  # descending sort: Gini > 0 = good model
  n   <- length(obs)
  ord <- order(pred, decreasing=TRUE)
  co  <- c(0, cumsum(obs[ord]) / sum(obs))
  cp  <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n+1)
  2 * sum(diff(cp) * (co[-1] + co[-(n+1)]) / 2) - 1
}
ln_backtr      <- function(pred_log, sigma) exp(pred_log + sigma^2 / 2)
ll_lognormal   <- function(y, mu_log, sigma)
  sum(dnorm(log(y), mean=mu_log, sd=sigma, log=TRUE) - log(y))
cv_summary     <- function(tbl) list(
  mean = colMeans(tbl, na.rm=TRUE),
  se   = apply(tbl, 2, function(x) sd(x, na.rm=TRUE)/sqrt(sum(!is.na(x)))))

# Consolidated severity metrics (return named vector).
# pred_log: raw log-scale LN predictions for LN_Dev = RSS on the log scale.
#           When NULL (Gamma model), LN_Dev falls back to ln_dev(y, pred).
# r2:       r2_cor(obs, pred) on the original scale for all models and splits.
sev_metrics <- function(y, pred, r2, aic, bic, pred_log = NULL) {
  ln_dev_val <- if (!is.null(pred_log))
                  sum((log(y) - pred_log)^2, na.rm = TRUE)
                else
                  ln_dev(y, pred)
  c(R2        = round(r2, 4),
    RMSE      = round(rmse(y, pred), 2),
    MAE       = round(mae(y, pred),  2),
    Gamma_Dev = round(gamma_dev(y, pred), 1),
    LN_Dev    = round(ln_dev_val, 2),
    Gini      = round(gini_index(y, pred), 4),
    AIC       = round(aic, 0),
    BIC       = round(bic, 0))
}

# AIC/BIC from a fitted LN model on original y scale (includes Jacobian)
ln_aic_bic <- function(y, mu_log, sigma, p, n) {
  ll  <- ll_lognormal(y, mu_log, sigma)
  k   <- p + 1    # betas + sigma
  c(AIC = -2*ll + 2*k, BIC = -2*ll + log(n)*k)
}

# AIC/BIC on test data using training-estimated parameters (Gamma)
gamma_test_aic_bic <- function(y, pred, phi, k, n) {
  shape <- 1/phi
  ll    <- sum(dgamma(y, shape=shape, rate=shape/pred, log=TRUE))
  c(AIC = -2*ll + 2*k, BIC = -2*ll + log(n)*k)
}

# Logistic test AIC/BIC
logistic_test_aic_bic <- function(y_bin, pred, k, n) {
  ll <- -binom_dev(y_bin, pred) / 2
  c(AIC = -2*ll + 2*k, BIC = -2*ll + log(n)*k)
}


# =============================================================================
# H: GOODNESS-OF-FIT TEST HELPER
# =============================================================================

# type: "gamma" | "lognormal" | "logistic"
run_gof_tests <- function(fit, std_res, type=c("gamma","lognormal","logistic")) {
  type <- match.arg(type)

  # --- Pearson Chi-Squared ---
  pears_stat <- switch(type,
    gamma     = sum(residuals(fit, type="pearson")^2),
    lognormal = sum(residuals(fit)^2) / sigma(fit)^2,
    logistic  = sum(residuals(fit, type="pearson")^2))
  df_p   <- fit$df.residual
  pv_p   <- 1 - pchisq(pears_stat, df=df_p)

  # --- Wald Test ---
  n_coef  <- length(coef(fit))
  wald    <- aod::wald.test(Sigma=vcov(fit), b=coef(fit), Terms=2:n_coef)
  w_stat  <- wald$result$chi2["chi2"]
  w_df    <- wald$result$chi2["df"]
  w_pv    <- wald$result$chi2["P"]

  # --- KS | AD | CvM ---
  ks  <- ks.test(std_res, "pnorm", 0, 1)
  ad  <- nortest::ad.test(std_res)
  cvm <- goftest::cvm.test(std_res, "pnorm")

  data.frame(
    Test      = c("Pearson Chi-Sq","Wald Test","KS Test","Anderson-Darling","Cramer-von Mises"),
    Statistic = round(c(pears_stat, w_stat, ks$statistic, ad$statistic, cvm$statistic), 4),
    DF        = c(df_p, w_df, NA, NA, NA),
    P_Value   = round(c(pv_p, w_pv, ks$p.value, ad$p.value, cvm$p.value), 4),
    stringsAsFactors=FALSE)
}

# Lightweight GOF for VGAM Pareto (no Pearson/Wald via aod)
run_gof_vglm <- function(fit, y_actual, y_pred) {
  resid_std <- (y_actual - y_pred); resid_std <- (resid_std - mean(resid_std))/sd(resid_std)
  ks  <- ks.test(resid_std, "pnorm", 0, 1)
  ad  <- nortest::ad.test(resid_std)
  cvm <- goftest::cvm.test(resid_std, "pnorm")
  wald_stat <- wald_pv <- NA
  tryCatch({
    nc <- length(coef(fit))
    w  <- aod::wald.test(Sigma=vcov(fit), b=coef(fit), Terms=2:nc)
    wald_stat <- w$result$chi2["chi2"]; wald_pv <- w$result$chi2["P"]
  }, error=function(e) NULL)
  data.frame(
    Test      = c("Wald Test","KS Test","Anderson-Darling","Cramer-von Mises"),
    Statistic = round(c(wald_stat, ks$statistic, ad$statistic, cvm$statistic), 4),
    P_Value   = round(c(wald_pv,   ks$p.value,   ad$p.value,   cvm$p.value),   4),
    stringsAsFactors=FALSE)
}


# =============================================================================
# I: PLOT HELPERS  (ggplot2)
# =============================================================================

th <- theme_minimal(base_size=11) +
  theme(plot.title=element_text(face="bold", size=11))

p_resid_v_fit <- function(fv, rv, lbl)
  ggplot(data.frame(x=fv,y=rv), aes(x,y)) +
    geom_point(size=.5, alpha=.3, colour="steelblue") +
    geom_hline(yintercept=0, linetype="dashed", colour="red") +
    geom_smooth(method="loess", se=FALSE, colour="orange", linewidth=.7, formula=y~x) +
    labs(title=paste("Residuals vs Fitted —",lbl), x="Fitted", y="Deviance Residuals") + th

p_hist_resid <- function(rv, lbl)
  ggplot(data.frame(r=rv), aes(r)) +
    geom_histogram(aes(y=after_stat(density)), bins=40, fill="steelblue",
                   alpha=.6, colour="white") +
    stat_function(fun=dnorm, args=list(mean=mean(rv),sd=sd(rv)),
                  colour="red", linewidth=.9) +
    labs(title=paste("Residuals Histogram —",lbl), x="Deviance Residuals", y="Density") + th

p_qq <- function(rv, lbl)
  ggplot(data.frame(r=rv), aes(sample=r)) +
    stat_qq(size=.6, alpha=.45, colour="steelblue") + stat_qq_line(colour="red", linewidth=.8) +
    labs(title=paste("Q-Q Plot —",lbl), x="Theoretical", y="Sample") + th

p_pp <- function(sv, lbl) {  # sv = standardised residuals
  n <- length(sv); s <- sort(sv)
  ggplot(data.frame(th=pnorm(s,0,1), em=(seq_len(n)-.5)/n), aes(th,em)) +
    geom_point(size=.5, alpha=.4, colour="steelblue") +
    geom_abline(slope=1, intercept=0, colour="red", linetype="dashed") +
    labs(title=paste("P-P Plot —",lbl), x="Theoretical P", y="Empirical P") + th
}

p_ave <- function(act, pred, lbl)
  ggplot(data.frame(p=pred,a=act), aes(p,a)) +
    geom_point(size=.5, alpha=.3, colour="steelblue") +
    geom_abline(slope=1, intercept=0, colour="red", linetype="dashed", linewidth=.8) +
    geom_smooth(method="loess", se=FALSE, colour="orange", linewidth=.8, formula=y~x) +
    labs(title=paste("AvE Plot (Test) —",lbl), x="Expected", y="Actual") + th

p_lift <- function(act, pred, lbl, nd=10) {
  # 1. Create the summary data
  df <- data.frame(a=act, p=pred) %>%
    mutate(dec=dplyr::ntile(p, nd)) %>%
    group_by(dec) %>%
    summarise(Actual=mean(a), Predicted=mean(p), .groups="drop")
  
  # 2. Reshape for ggplot
  dl <- data.frame(
    Dec = rep(df$dec, 2),
    Val = c(df$Actual, df$Predicted),
    Ser = rep(c("Actual", "Predicted"), each=nrow(df))
  )
  
  # 3. Plot with Bars AND Lines
  ggplot(dl, aes(x = factor(Dec), y = Val, fill = Ser, group = Ser, color = Ser)) +
    # The Bars
    geom_bar(stat="identity", position="dodge", width=.7, alpha = 0.6) +
    # The Connecting Lines
    geom_line(size = 1) + 
    # The Points on the lines
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    scale_fill_manual(values=c(Actual="steelblue", Predicted="coral")) +
    scale_color_manual(values=c(Actual="steelblue", Predicted="coral")) +
    labs(title=paste("Lift Chart (Test) —", lbl),
         x="Decile (Low→High Predicted)", y="Mean Cost", fill="", color="") + 
    th +
    theme(legend.position="bottom")
}

save_diag_4 <- function(p1,p2,p3,p4, file, title_str) {
  pdf(here(file), width=11, height=8.5)
  gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1,p2,p3,p4, ncol=2,
    top=grid::textGrob(title_str, gp=grid::gpar(fontsize=14, fontface="bold")))
  dev.off(); cat("Saved:", file, "\n")
}

fmt_cv <- function(m, s, d=3) sprintf(paste0("%.",d,"f (\u00b1%.",d,"f)"), m, s)

print_metrics_tbl <- function(tr_vec, te_vec, model_name, caption_str) {
  tbl <- data.frame(Metric=names(tr_vec), Train=tr_vec, Test=te_vec,
                    stringsAsFactors=FALSE)
  cat("\n===", caption_str, "===\n")
  print(kable(tbl, caption=caption_str,
              col.names=c("Metric", paste(model_name,"Train"),
                          paste(model_name,"Test")),
              align=c("l","c","c"), row.names=FALSE))
}


# =============================================================================
# J: SHARED FULL-CLAIMANT ENCODING
# =============================================================================

enc_sev   <- encode_predictors(train_sev, test_sev, target)
X_tr_sev  <- enc_sev$train;  X_te_sev  <- enc_sev$test
y_tr_sev  <- train_sev$avg_claim_cost
y_te_sev  <- test_sev$avg_claim_cost
n_tr_sev  <- length(y_tr_sev);  n_te_sev <- length(y_te_sev)
ext_tr    <- y_tr_sev > THRESHOLD;  ext_te <- y_te_sev > THRESHOLD

cat(sprintf("Attrition train: %d | Extreme train: %d\nAttrition test : %d | Extreme test : %d\n",
            sum(!ext_tr), sum(ext_tr), sum(!ext_te), sum(ext_te)))

# =============================================================================
# 0: CROSS-VALIDATION EXECUTION (Pre-calculation)
# =============================================================================
cat("\n--- Running Cross-Validation: Traditional Models ---\n")

cv_trad <- lapply(cv_folds$splits, function(sp) {
  tr <- analysis(sp)   %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
  va <- assessment(sp) %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
  if (nrow(tr) == 0 || nrow(va) == 0) return(NULL)
  
  enc <- encode_predictors(tr, va, target)
  ytr <- tr$avg_claim_cost; yva <- va$avg_claim_cost
  
  # Gamma
  m_g   <- tryCatch(fit_glm(ytr, enc$train, Gamma(link="log")), error=function(e) NULL)
  p_g   <- if (!is.null(m_g)) predict(m_g, enc$test, type="response") else rep(mean(ytr), length(yva))
  phi_g <- summary(m_g)$dispersion
  k_g   <- length(coef(m_g)) + 1   # +1 for dispersion parameter
  n_g   <- length(yva)
  ab_g  <- gamma_test_aic_bic(yva, p_g, phi_g, k_g, n_g)

  # Log-Normal: pred_log_l = raw log-scale predictions; p_l = bias-corrected original-scale
  m_l        <- tryCatch(fit_lm(log(ytr), enc$train), error=function(e) NULL)
  sg         <- if (!is.null(m_l)) sigma(m_l) else sd(log(ytr))
  pred_log_l <- if (!is.null(m_l)) predict(m_l, enc$test) else rep(mean(log(ytr)), length(yva))
  p_l        <- ln_backtr(pred_log_l, sg)
  k_l        <- length(coef(m_l))
  n_l        <- length(yva)
  ab_l       <- ln_aic_bic(yva, pred_log_l, sg, k_l, n_l)

  data.frame(
    Gamma_R2   = r2_cor(yva, p_g),
    Gamma_RMSE = rmse(yva, p_g),
    Gamma_MAE  = mae(yva, p_g),
    Gamma_GDev = gamma_dev(yva, p_g),
    Gamma_Gini = gini_index(yva, p_g),
    Gamma_AIC  = ab_g["AIC"],
    Gamma_BIC  = ab_g["BIC"],

    LN_R2   = r2_cor(yva, p_l),
    LN_RMSE = rmse(yva, p_l),
    LN_MAE  = mae(yva, p_l),
    LN_Dev  = sum((log(yva) - pred_log_l)^2),   # RSS on the log scale
    LN_Gini = gini_index(yva, p_l),
    LN_AIC  = ab_l["AIC"],
    LN_BIC  = ab_l["BIC"]
  )})

cv_trad_tbl <- bind_rows(Filter(Negate(is.null), cv_trad))
cv_trad_s   <- cv_summary(cv_trad_tbl) # Assuming this returns mean and se

```


# Exploring Relationship between Predictors and Targets

## Correlation

Spearman rank correlations between each predictor and avg_claim_cost are computed separately for the attritional and extreme claimant subsets. Using Spearman rather than Pearson correlation is deliberate: the target is heavily right-skewed and the predictors are a mix of ordinal bands and nominal categories encoded as integers, so a rank-based measure is more robust to the distributional shape of both variables. 

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='left'}
# ----------------------------------------------------------
# B) TARGET vs FEATURE CORRPLOT (avg_claim_cost only)
# ----------------------------------------------------------

# 1. Prepare the Severity-only dataframe
severity_df <- basic_glm_data_no_na

# 2. Add the classification
severity_df$severity_classification <- ifelse(severity_df$avg_claim_cost > benchmark, 
                                              "extreme", 
                                              "attritional")

# Define the single target variable for the Y-axis
target_var <- "avg_claim_cost"

# Define all variables that are 'results' rather than 'predictors'
final_target_vars <- c(
  "n_claims_year",
  "avg_claim_cost",
  "lapse_flag",
  "days_to_event",
  "severity_classification"
)

# Define variables to exclude (IDs, exposure, and targets)
feature_vars <- setdiff(
  names(severity_df), # Use the updated dataframe
  c("id", "id2", "exposure_years", "premium_band", "premium_vs_median_band", final_target_vars)
)

severity_numeric <- severity_df 

for (v in feature_vars) {
  if (is.factor(severity_numeric[[v]])) {
    severity_numeric[[v]] <- as.numeric(severity_numeric[[v]])
  }
  
  if (is.character(severity_numeric[[v]])) {
    severity_numeric[[v]] <- as.numeric(factor(severity_numeric[[v]]))
  }
}

# --- NOW you can split and calculate correlations ---

# 1. Split the NOW-NUMERIC data into two subsets
attrit_data <- severity_numeric[severity_numeric$severity_classification == "attritional", ]
extreme_data <- severity_numeric[severity_numeric$severity_classification == "extreme", ]

# 2. Create the matrix
target_names <- c("Attritional Severity", "Extreme Severity")
target_feature_matrix <- matrix(
  nrow = 2,
  ncol = length(feature_vars),
  dimnames = list(target_names, feature_vars)
)

# 3. Run the correlation loop
for (f in feature_vars) {
  target_feature_matrix[1, f] <- cor(attrit_data[[target_var]], attrit_data[[f]], 
                                     method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
  
  target_feature_matrix[2, f] <- cor(extreme_data[[target_var]], extreme_data[[f]], 
                                     method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
}

# 4. Plot the comparison
corrplot(
  target_feature_matrix,
  is.corr = FALSE,
  method = "color",
  tl.col = "black",
  tl.cex = 0.7,
  tl.srt = 90,       # 45 is often easier to read than 90 for long feature names
  cl.pos = "n",      # Put legend on the right for a 2-row plot
  col = colorRampPalette(c("#2166AC", "white", "#B2182B"))(200),
  mar = c(1, 1, 3, 1),
  title = "Spearman Correlation: Red (+ve) and Blue (-ve)"
)
```

```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# VARIABLE TAXONOMY
# =============================================================================

one_hot_vars <- c(
  "area_label", "channel_label", "payment_label", "second_driver_label",
  "type_fuel", "seniority_category", "start_season", "start_day_of_week"
)

ord_vars <- c(
  "age_category", "age_when_licensed_band", "driving_exp_category",
  "vehicle_age_category", "power_band", "cylinder_band", "value_band",
  "weight_band", "length_imputed_band", "n_doors_band",
  # "claims_history_band"       # dropped: near-perfect collinearity
  # "r_claims_history_band"     # dropped: near-perfect collinearity
  "avg_claims_per_year_history_band", "cross_sell_ratio_band",
  "policy_retention_rate_band", "risk_score_band"
)

binary_vars <- c(
  "growing_portfolio", "had_previous_lapse", "multi_policy_holder",
  "multi_product_holder", "start_weekend"
)

cat(sprintf(
  "Predictor features: %d total  (%d nominal OHE | %d ordinal | %d binary)\n",
  length(one_hot_vars) + length(ord_vars) + length(binary_vars),
  length(one_hot_vars), length(ord_vars), length(binary_vars)
))

```

## Standard Deviation from Mean (0)

To make category-level patterns comparable across variables with very different cost scales, group means are standardised to z-scores within each severity classification. A bar sitting above zero indicates that the category is associated with above-average claim costs within that segment; a bar below zero indicates below-average. Plotting attritional and extreme bars side by side for each category reveals where the two segments agree (bars pointing in the same direction) and where they diverge (sign reversal), providing an intuitive visual overview of the variables that may be differentially predictive of extreme versus attritional outcomes.


### Nominal Variables

```{r, echo=FALSE}
plot_zscore_severity <- function(data, var, target = "avg_claim_cost") {
  
  # 1. Calculate means per category and class
  summary_df <- data %>%
    group_by(!!sym(var), severity_classification) %>%
    summarise(mean_sev = mean(!!sym(target), na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  
  # 2. Apply Z-score Standardization within each classification
  # (Value - Mean) / Standard Deviation
  summary_df <- summary_df %>%
    group_by(severity_classification) %>%
    mutate(
      z_sev = (mean_sev - mean(mean_sev)) / sd(mean_sev)
    ) %>%
    ungroup()

  # 3. Plot
  ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = factor(!!sym(var)), y = z_sev, fill = severity_classification)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", size = 0.5) + # Baseline at 0
    geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("attritional" = "#377eb8", "extreme" = "#e41a1c")) +
    labs(
      title = paste(var),
      x = NULL, 
      y = NULL,
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
      legend.position = "none"
    )
}

# Apply using your updated severity_df
ohe_vs_target_plots <- lapply(one_hot_vars, function(v) {
  plot_zscore_severity(severity_df, v)
})

# Wrap the plots
library(patchwork)
wrap_plots(ohe_vs_target_plots) + 
  plot_layout(ncol = 4, nrow = 2, guides = "collect") & 
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


### Ordinal Variables

```{r, echo=FALSE}
plot_ord_vs_target_zscore <- function(data, var, target = "avg_claim_cost") {
  
  # 1. Calculate means per category and class
  summary_df <- data %>%
    group_by(!!sym(var), severity_classification) %>%
    summarise(mean_sev = mean(!!sym(target), na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  
  # 2. Apply Z-score Standardization within each classification
  summary_df <- summary_df %>%
    group_by(severity_classification) %>%
    mutate(z_sev = (mean_sev - mean(mean_sev)) / sd(mean_sev)) %>%
    ungroup()

  # 3. Plotting with a line to emphasize the ordinal trend
  ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = factor(!!sym(var)), y = z_sev, fill = severity_classification)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
    # We keep the bars for consistency with your OHE plots
    geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.7) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("attritional" = "#377eb8", "extreme" = "#e41a1c")) +
    labs(
      title = var, 
      x = NULL, 
      y = NULL,
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 6, angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1),
      plot.title = element_text(size = 8, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
    )
}

# Apply using the severity_df and your ordinal variables list
ord_vs_target_plots <- lapply(ord_vars, function(v) {
  plot_ord_vs_target_zscore(severity_df, v)
})

# Wrap the plots into a grid
library(patchwork)
wrap_plots(ord_vs_target_plots) + 
  plot_layout(ncol = 5, nrow = 3, guides = "collect") & 
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


### Binary Variables

```{r, echo=FALSE}
plot_binary_vs_target_zscore <- function(data, var, target = "avg_claim_cost") {
  
  # 1. Calculate means per category (0/1 or T/F) and class
  summary_df <- data %>%
    group_by(!!sym(var), severity_classification) %>%
    summarise(mean_sev = mean(!!sym(target), na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  
  # 2. Apply Z-score Standardization within each classification
  summary_df <- summary_df %>%
    group_by(severity_classification) %>%
    mutate(z_sev = (mean_sev - mean(mean_sev)) / sd(mean_sev)) %>%
    ungroup()

  # 3. Plot with symmetrical bars
  ggplot(summary_df, aes(x = factor(!!sym(var)), y = z_sev, fill = severity_classification)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
    geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.6) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("attritional" = "#377eb8", "extreme" = "#e41a1c")) +
    labs(
      title = var, 
      x = NULL, 
      y = NULL
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
    theme(
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 7),
      plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5)
    )
}

# Apply to binary variables
binary_vs_target_plots <- lapply(binary_vars, function(v) {
  plot_binary_vs_target_zscore(severity_df, v)
})

# Wrap the plots
wrap_plots(binary_vs_target_plots) + 
  plot_layout(ncol = 3, nrow = 2, guides = "collect") & 
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```


# Gamma Model

The Gamma GLM with a log link is the actuarial industry standard for modelling claim severity. The Gamma family is chosen because it is defined on the positive real line, assumes a coefficient of variation that is constant across the range of fitted values (variance proportional to the square of the mean), and the log link ensures multiplicative effects on the cost scale — all properties consistent with insurance claim costs. The model is fitted on the claimant-only subsample (policies with n_claims_year > 0), since severity modelling conditions on a claim having occurred.



```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# K: TRADITIONAL — GAMMA GLM
# =============================================================================
cat("\n\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70)); cat("\nTRADITIONAL: GAMMA GLM\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70), "\n")

gamma_fit <- fit_glm(y_tr_sev, X_tr_sev, Gamma(link="log"))
cat("\n--- MODEL SUMMARY ---\n"); print(summary(gamma_fit))



cat("\n--- PER-FOLD CV RESULTS (Gamma) ---\n")
print(cv_trad_tbl %>% dplyr::select(starts_with("Gamma")))

# Metric Calculation
fv_gamma    <- fitted(gamma_fit)
phi_gamma   <- summary(gamma_fit)$dispersion
k_gamma     <- length(coef(gamma_fit)) + 1
tr_gamma    <- sev_metrics(y_tr_sev, fv_gamma,
                           r2_cor(y_tr_sev, fv_gamma),
                           AIC(gamma_fit), BIC(gamma_fit))

pred_gamma_te <- predict(gamma_fit, X_te_sev, type="response")
te_ab_gamma   <- gamma_test_aic_bic(y_te_sev, pred_gamma_te, phi_gamma, k_gamma, n_te_sev)
te_gamma <- sev_metrics(
  y_te_sev,
  pred_gamma_te,
  r2_cor(y_te_sev, pred_gamma_te),
  te_ab_gamma[["AIC"]],
  te_ab_gamma[["BIC"]]
)

cat("\n--- CV MEAN +/- S.E. VS. TEST SCORES (Gamma) ---\n")
gamma_cv_vs_te <- data.frame(
  Metric  = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma_Dev", "Gini"),
  CV_Mean = unname(cv_trad_s$mean[c("Gamma_R2","Gamma_RMSE","Gamma_MAE","Gamma_GDev","Gamma_Gini")]),
  CV_SE   = unname(cv_trad_s$se[c("Gamma_R2","Gamma_RMSE","Gamma_MAE","Gamma_GDev","Gamma_Gini")]),
  Test    = unname(te_gamma[c("R2", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma_Dev", "Gini")])
)
print(gamma_cv_vs_te)

# Preparation for Diagnostics
dev_res_gamma <- residuals(gamma_fit, type="deviance")
std_res_gamma <- (dev_res_gamma - mean(dev_res_gamma)) / sd(dev_res_gamma)



```
\underline{Discussion:}

The model identifies a small but interpretable set of significant predictors:

- Age category shows the strongest and most consistent effect, with all age bands above 18–25 having similar positive coefficients (≈ 0.63–0.70).
- Claimants aged 26+ incur roughly **exp(0.64) ≈ 1.90×** the claim cost of the youngest group.
- The effect is stable across age bands and aligns with older drivers having **higher‑cost** claims despite lower claim frequency.

Vehicle age acts in the opposite direction:

- Vehicles aged 4–7 years show a coefficient of **−0.25** relative to new vehicles (0–3 years).
- Vehicles aged 8–12 and 12+ years show coefficients of **−0.39** and **−0.45**, respectively.
- Older vehicles therefore generate **lower average claim costs**, consistent with lower replacement value.

Vehicle value band shows a clear positive gradient:

- The highest value band (€32,500+) has a coefficient of **0.39 (p = 0.001)**.
- This reflects that more expensive vehicles cost more to repair or replace.

Prior lapse history contributes a meaningful cost loading:

- A previous lapse adds approximately **0.25** to the log cost (p = 0.001).
- Indicates a persistent severity effect beyond the frequency‑based selection of claimants.

High risk score band (0.2+) shows a large but imprecise effect:

- Coefficient of **0.61 (p = 0.015)**, the largest in the model.
- Wide standard error (0.249) reflects sparsity in this band.

Most remaining predictors are not statistically significant:

- Cylinder, weight, length, doors, seniority, seasonal, and distribution channel variables show no meaningful effects.
- Suggests claim severity is driven by a **narrower set of structural factors** than claim frequency.

The dispersion parameter indicates substantial variability:

- Dispersion of **8.13** implies a Gamma shape parameter of **≈ 0.12**, close to an exponential distribution.
- Null deviance reduces only modestly (29,189 → 27,829), meaning the model explains **~4.7%** of total variation.

Cross‑validation results show low but stable predictive power:

- R² ranges from **0.0015 to 0.0051** (mean 0.0032 +/- 0.0006).
- Gini ranges from **0.080 to 0.123** (mean 0.104 +/- 0.007).
- RMSE varies widely (1,020–2,393) due to the presence or absence of a few very large claims in each fold.

Hold‑out test performance aligns with CV expectations:

- Test R² = **0.0070** and Gini = **0.1051**, both within the CV range.
- Test RMSE = **1,102**, lower than the CV mean (1,609), driven by fold‑level variance rather than overfitting.
- Test Gamma deviance = **6,988**, higher than the CV mean (5,796), though CV deviance is based on smaller fold sizes, limiting comparability.


# Log-Normal Model

The Log-Normal model fits an ordinary least squares regression on the natural logarithm of claim cost, exploiting the approximate normality of log(avg_claim_cost) that is visible in the zoomed distribution. Predictions are returned to the original cost scale using the smearing back-transformation exp(μ̂ + σ²/2), which corrects for the Jensen's inequality bias that would otherwise cause exp(μ̂) to systematically underestimate the conditional mean. The residual standard deviation σ is estimated from the training data and held fixed when generating test set predictions.


```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# L: TRADITIONAL — LOG-NORMAL
# =============================================================================
cat("\n\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70)); cat("\nTRADITIONAL: LOG-NORMAL\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70), "\n")

ln_fit    <- fit_lm(log(y_tr_sev), X_tr_sev)
sigma_ln  <- sigma(ln_fit)

fv_ln_log <- fitted(ln_fit)                   # raw log-scale fitted values
fv_ln     <- ln_backtr(fv_ln_log, sigma_ln)   # bias-corrected original-scale fitted values
p_ln      <- length(coef(ln_fit))

ab_tr_ln  <- ln_aic_bic(y_tr_sev, fv_ln_log, sigma_ln, p_ln, n_tr_sev)

tr_ln <- sev_metrics(
  y        = y_tr_sev,
  pred     = fv_ln,
  r2       = r2_cor(y_tr_sev, fv_ln),
  aic      = ab_tr_ln[["AIC"]],
  bic      = ab_tr_ln[["BIC"]],
  pred_log = fv_ln_log
)

cat("\n--- MODEL SUMMARY ---\n"); print(summary(ln_fit))

# Test metrics
pred_ln_log_te <- predict(ln_fit, X_te_sev)              # raw log-scale test predictions
pred_ln_te     <- ln_backtr(pred_ln_log_te, sigma_ln)    # bias-corrected for RMSE/MAE/Gini

ab_te_ln <- { ll <- ll_lognormal(y_te_sev, pred_ln_log_te, sigma_ln)
              c(AIC = -2*ll + 2*(p_ln+1), BIC = -2*ll + log(n_te_sev)*(p_ln+1)) }

te_ln <- sev_metrics(
  y        = y_te_sev,
  pred     = pred_ln_te,
  r2       = r2_cor(y_te_sev, pred_ln_te),
  aic      = ab_te_ln[["AIC"]],
  bic      = ab_te_ln[["BIC"]],
  pred_log = pred_ln_log_te
)

cat("\n--- CV MEAN +/- S.E. VS. TEST SCORES (Log-Normal) ---\n")
ln_cv_vs_te <- data.frame(
  Metric  = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "LN_Dev [RSS_log]", "Gini"),
  CV_Mean = unname(cv_trad_s$mean[c("LN_R2","LN_RMSE","LN_MAE","LN_Dev","LN_Gini")]),
  CV_SE   = unname(cv_trad_s$se[c("LN_R2","LN_RMSE","LN_MAE","LN_Dev","LN_Gini")]),
  Test    = unname(te_ln[c("R2", "RMSE", "MAE", "LN_Dev", "Gini")])
)
print(ln_cv_vs_te)

# Preparation for Diagnostics
res_ln     <- residuals(ln_fit)
std_res_ln <- res_ln / sigma_ln


```
\underline{Discussion:}

The Log-Normal model shows consistently stronger predictive performance than the Gamma:

- CV R² averages **0.0051 (+/-0.0018)**, higher than the Gamma’s **0.0032**.
- CV Gini averages **0.110 (+/-0.010)**, again above the Gamma’s **0.104**.
- These improvements appear consistent across folds rather than driven by the test split.

The corrected Log-Normal deviance behaves as expected:

- LN_Dev (RSS on the log scale) averages **5,025** per fold (+/-70).
- This scales proportionally to **6,195** on the full test set.
- The metric is now correctly defined as the sum of squared log‑residuals.

Hold-out test performance reinforces the Log-Normal advantage:

- Test R² increases to **0.0118**, roughly double the Gamma’s **0.0070**.
- Test Gini rises to **0.1462**, again nearly double the Gamma’s **0.1051**.
- Test MAE = **443.8**, lower than the Gamma’s **470.0**, indicating better median accuracy across the bulk of claims.



# Training Evaluation

The training metrics serve as a baseline to assess in-sample fit and to anchor the comparison with cross-validation and test performance. Two deviance metrics are reported for each model: Gamma_Dev (= 2Σ(y/ŷ − log(y/ŷ) − 1), the scaled deviance of the Gamma GLM) and LN_Dev (= Σ(log y − log ŷ)², the residual sum of squares on the log scale that the Log-Normal model directly minimises). These are fundamentally different functions and are reported separately for transparency — they should not be compared across model columns. All R² figures throughout this document are computed as the squared Pearson correlation between observed and fitted values on the original cost scale, cor(y, ŷ)², ensuring the metric is directly comparable across the Gamma and Log-Normal models and across training, cross-validation, and test splits.



```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# # Training Evaluation
# =============================================================================
cat("\n\n# Training Evaluation\n")

train_eval_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric     = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma_Dev", "LN_Dev [RSS_log]", "Gini"),
  Gamma_GLM  = unname(tr_gamma[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")]),
  Log_Normal = unname(tr_ln[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")])
)
print(train_eval_tbl)
```
\underline{Discussion:}

The Gamma shows slightly stronger in-sample correlation but does not generalise as well:
- Training R²: Gamma **0.0087** vs Log-Normal **0.0044**, reflecting Gamma’s flexibility on the original scale.
- This advantage disappears out of sample: test R² reverses to Gamma **0.0070** vs Log-Normal **0.0118**, indicating mild overfitting by the Gamma.

The Log-Normal provides better in-sample accuracy on the median scale:
- Training MAE: Log-Normal **461.2** vs Gamma **484.3**, meaning LN back-transformed predictions sit closer to observed costs on average.

Log-domain fit clearly favours the Log-Normal:
- LN_Dev (RSS on log scale) = **24,904**, the actual quantity minimised by the Log-Normal.
- Applying the same log-squared-error metric to Gamma predictions yields **38,218**, confirming the Log-Normal’s superior fit in the log domain.

The Gamma ranks claimants better in-sample but overfits this ordering:
- Training Gini: Gamma **0.1715** vs Log-Normal **0.1249**, indicating stronger in-sample ranking performance.
- This reverses on the test set: Gamma **0.1051** vs Log-Normal **0.1462**, a clear sign that the Gamma’s ranking relies on training-specific noise.


# Test Evaluation

The hold-out test set — comprising 3,788 policies across 3,021 clients not seen during training — provides an unbiased estimate of generalisation performance. Because the test R² is computed on the same cor(y, ŷ)² basis as the training and CV figures, any deterioration between training and test directly reflects overfitting. 

```{r, echo=FALSE}
cat("\n\n# Test Evaluation\n")

# =============================================================================
# # Test Evaluation
# =============================================================================
test_eval_tbl <- data.frame(
  Metric     = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma_Dev", "LN_Dev [RSS_log]", "Gini"),
  Gamma_GLM  = unname(te_gamma[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")]),
  Log_Normal = unname(te_ln[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")])
)
print(test_eval_tbl)
```
\underline{Discussion:}

The test set provides the most reliable basis for comparing the two models:

- The Log-Normal outperforms the Gamma on all key metrics:  
  **R²** (0.0118 vs 0.0070), **MAE** (443.8 vs 470.0), **Gini** (0.1462 vs 0.1051), and **LN_Dev** (6,195 vs 9,646).
- These improvements indicate stronger discrimination, better calibration, and closer median predictions for the Log-Normal.

The Gamma shows substantial deterioration in ranking ability out of sample:

- Training Gini drops from **0.1715 → 0.1051**, a **39% decline**.
- The Log-Normal’s Gini declines more modestly (**0.1249 → 0.1462**, a 17% change in the opposite direction).
- This contrast suggests the Gamma overfits the severity ranking, while the Log-Normal generalises more robustly.

RMSE shows little difference between the models:

- Test RMSE is nearly identical (Gamma **1,102** vs Log-Normal **1,099**).
- This reflects RMSE being dominated by a few extreme claims that neither model captures well.

Gamma deviance provides a useful cross-diagnostic:

- The Log-Normal’s Gamma deviance (**6,866**) is slightly lower than the Gamma GLM’s own (**6,988**).
- This indicates that the Log-Normal’s original-scale predictions are at least as well-calibrated under a Gamma loss.

Overall, the Log-Normal is the preferred model based on test set performance.


# Goodness-of-Fit Tests

Five tests are applied to the standardised residuals of each model. The Pearson Chi-Squared test assesses whether the overall residual variance is consistent with the assumed distributional family; a statistic far exceeding the degrees of freedom signals distributional misspecification. The Wald test jointly tests whether the predictor coefficients are all zero, providing a global assessment of model utility. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov, Anderson-Darling, and Cramér-von Mises tests each assess whether the standardised residuals conform to a standard normal distribution — a necessary condition for the assumed error structure to hold. The Anderson-Darling test places greater weight on the tails and is therefore particularly informative for heavy-tailed residuals. 


```{r, echo=FALSE}
# =============================================================================
# # Goodness-of-Fit Tests
# =============================================================================
cat("\n\n# Goodness-of-Fit Tests\n")

cat("\n--- Gamma GLM (Train) ---\n")
gof_gamma <- run_gof_tests(gamma_fit, std_res_gamma, "gamma"); print(gof_gamma)

cat("\n--- Log-Normal (Train) ---\n")
gof_ln <- run_gof_tests(ln_fit, std_res_ln, "lognormal"); print(gof_ln)

# Visual Diagnostics
pg <- list(p_resid_v_fit(log(fv_gamma), dev_res_gamma, "Gamma GLM"),
           p_hist_resid(dev_res_gamma, "Gamma GLM"),
           p_qq(dev_res_gamma, "Gamma GLM"),
           p_pp(std_res_gamma, "Gamma GLM"))
save_diag_4(pg[[1]], pg[[2]], pg[[3]], pg[[4]], 
            "severity_diagnostics_train_gamma.pdf", "Gamma GLM — Training Diagnostics")

pl <- list(p_resid_v_fit(fv_ln_log, res_ln, "Log-Normal"),
           p_hist_resid(res_ln, "Log-Normal"),
           p_qq(res_ln, "Log-Normal"),
           p_pp(std_res_ln, "Log-Normal"))
save_diag_4(pl[[1]], pl[[2]], pl[[3]], pl[[4]], 
            "severity_diagnostics_train_lognormal.pdf", "Log-Normal — Training Diagnostics")
```

\underline{Discussion:}

The Gamma GLM shows severe distributional misspecification:

- Pearson Chi-Squared = **122,818** on 15,106 df, around **8×** the expected value, giving p = 0.
- This indicates the residual variance is eight times larger than the Gamma family assumes — a catastrophic failure of the distributional form.
- Normality tests on standardised residuals (KS, AD, CvM) all return p = 0.
- The Anderson–Darling statistic of **338.5** shows the right tail is dramatically heavier than the Gamma model allows.

The Log-Normal passes the key distributional checks:

- Pearson Chi-Squared = **15,106** on 15,106 df, p = 0.499 — exactly what is expected under a correctly specified model.
- This provides strong evidence that the log-normal assumption fits the bulk of the data well.

The predictor set is jointly significant under the Log-Normal:

- Wald test: χ² = **695.4** on 49 df, p = 0.
- This is far larger than the Gamma’s χ² = 154.4, reflecting a better-conditioned likelihood and stronger explanatory power.

Residual distribution tests still reject perfect normality for the Log-Normal:

- KS = **0.059**, AD = **67.1**, CvM = **12.1**, all p < 0.001.
- At n = 15,156, even small deviations trigger rejection, so this is expected.
- Crucially, the magnitudes are **dramatically smaller** than for the Gamma — AD drops from 338.5 to 67.1, a **fivefold improvement**.
- This indicates the Log-Normal residuals are substantially closer to the assumed normal form.



# Visual Diagnosis Plots

## Goodness-of-Fit Plots

Four paired diagnostic plots compare the Gamma GLM (left) and Log-Normal (right) on the training data. The Residuals vs Fitted plot should show points scattered randomly around zero with a flat LOESS smoother; any upward trend or fan shape indicates heteroscedasticity or mean misspecification. The Residuals Histogram overlays a fitted normal curve on the empirical residual density; a good fit implies the assumed error distribution is appropriate. The Q-Q plot maps empirical quantiles against theoretical normal quantiles; departure from the diagonal, especially in the tails, indicates distributional misfit. The P-P plot maps the empirical cumulative distribution against the theoretical; an S-shaped departure indicates the model systematically underestimates or overestimates residual probabilities in different regions of the distribution. 


```{r gof-diag-plots, fig.width=14, fig.height=6, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
# pg (Gamma) and pl (Log-Normal) were already built in the Goodness-of-Fit Tests
# chunk above. No need to rebuild pl or call save_diag_4 a second time here.

library(patchwork)

plot_types <- c("Residuals vs Fitted", "Histogram of Residuals", "Q-Q Plot", "P-P Plot")

for(i in 1:4) {
  combined_plot <- pg[[i]] + pl[[i]] +
    plot_annotation(
      title    = paste("Diagnostic Comparison:", plot_types[i]),
      subtitle = "Left: Gamma GLM | Right: Log-Normal"
    )
  print(combined_plot)
}
```


\underline{Discussion:}

Residuals vs Fitted plots highlight clear differences between the models:

- The Gamma plot shows deviance residuals fanning upward with fitted values, and a LOESS line that rises steadily — evidence that the assumed variance function (∝ μ²) fails to stabilise residuals.
- The Log-Normal plot shows a tighter band around zero and a flatter LOESS line, with only mild upward drift at high fitted values, indicating residual heteroscedasticity mainly in the extreme-cost region.

Histogram of residuals shows stark contrast in distributional fit:

- The Gamma histogram is sharply right-skewed, with mass near zero and a long positive tail extending beyond 20 standard deviations — completely inconsistent with the normal overlay.
- The Log-Normal histogram is approximately bell-shaped and aligns closely with the normal curve, with only slight right-tail asymmetry, consistent with its acceptable Pearson test result.

Q-Q plots reveal tail behaviour differences:

- The Gamma Q-Q plot diverges dramatically from the diagonal beyond theoretical quantile 2, with sample quantiles reaching ~20 vs theoretical ~4 — a **5:1 tail inflation**.
- The Log-Normal Q-Q plot tracks the diagonal through the centre, with mild S-shaped deviation at both ends.  
  The positive tail reaches ~5 vs theoretical ~4, reflecting underestimation of extreme claims — consistent with a possible two-regime severity structure.

P-P plots show similar qualitative patterns but different magnitudes:

- Both models display an S-shaped deviation: empirical CDF lags in the lower tail, overshoots in the mid-range, and reconverges in the upper tail — the signature of a distribution that is slightly too peaked and heavy-tailed relative to normality.
- The Log-Normal’s S-curve is **much shallower** than the Gamma’s, indicating a better-calibrated probability structure across the full residual range.


## Predictive Performance Plots

Two further paired plots evaluate out-of-sample predictive performance on the test set. The Actual vs Expected (AvE) plot plots observed costs against model predictions at the individual policy level; the ideal is a cloud of points distributed symmetrically around the 45° diagonal. Systematic departure from the diagonal — for example, the orange LOESS line lying consistently above or below — indicates directional bias in the predictions. The Lift chart ranks test policies into ten deciles by predicted cost (low to high) and compares the mean actual cost per decile against the mean predicted cost. A well-calibrated model produces actual and predicted bars that track closely across all deciles, and a monotonically increasing actual line confirms that higher predictions genuinely correspond to higher observed costs. The Log-Normal model shows smoother separation and tighter calibration across deciles, consistent with its superior Gini coefficient on the test set.

```{r traditional-ave-lift, fig.width=14, fig.height=10, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE}
library(patchwork)

# 1. Generate the individual plots
p1 <- p_ave(y_te_sev, pred_gamma_te, "Gamma GLM")
p2 <- p_ave(y_te_sev, pred_ln_te,    "Log-Normal")
p3 <- p_lift(y_te_sev, pred_gamma_te, "Gamma GLM")
p4 <- p_lift(y_te_sev, pred_ln_te,    "Log-Normal")


# 1. Pair up the AvE plots (Gamma vs Log-Normal)
ave_combined <- p1 + p2 + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Diagnostic Comparison: Actual vs Expected",
    subtitle = "Left: Gamma GLM | Right: Log-Normal",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )

# 2. Pair up the Lift plots (Gamma vs Log-Normal)
lift_combined <- p3 + p4 + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Diagnostic Comparison: Lift Charts",
    subtitle = "Left: Gamma GLM | Right: Log-Normal",
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"))
  )

# 3. Print them
print(ave_combined)
print(lift_combined)
```

\underline{Discussion:}

The AvE plots highlight the limits of point prediction for both models:

- In both Gamma and Log-Normal, the scatter extends vertically up to **€20,000** while fitted values remain tightly compressed  
  (Gamma: €200–€1,600; Log-Normal: €200–€900).
- This reflects extreme claim heterogeneity that neither model can capture with mean predictions.
- The LOESS line in both plots sits slightly above the 45° reference line, indicating **systematic underestimation** of average actual costs — consistent with heavy-tail exposure that single-distribution GLMs smooth over.

Lift charts show clearer separation and stability for the Log-Normal:

- The Gamma lift chart is erratic in the lower and middle deciles (especially deciles 2–5), with actual and predicted lines crossing multiple times before aligning in the top decile.
- The Log-Normal lift chart shows smoother, more monotonic behaviour, with actual and predicted values tracking more consistently across all deciles.
- In both models, the top decile shows predicted means (~€600–€650) well below actual means (~€800), reflecting **persistent underprediction of the highest-severity segment** — a structural limitation of single-distribution GLM severity models under extreme right tails.

---

# Hurdle Frameworks

The hurdle framework decomposes the severity distribution into two distinct stages. In the first stage, a binary classifier identifies whether a claimant's cost exceeds the extreme threshold of €900 — the boundary established in the data exploration section, at or below which approximately 91% of claimants fall. In the second stage, separate distributional models are fitted independently to each segment: attritional claims (avg_claim_cost ≤ €900) and extreme claims (avg_claim_cost > €900). This stratified approach allows each sub-model to exploit within-segment distributional structure more effectively than a single-distribution model applied to the full, heavy-tailed severity distribution. The binary classification component uses the same full-claimant training set as the traditional models. All severity components use segment-specific training subsets, with encoding, cross-validation structure, and train–test split inherited from the same infrastructure established in the Data Pre-processing section.


```{r hurdle-precomp, include=FALSE}

# =============================================================================
# HURDLE FRAMEWORK PRE-COMPUTATION
# Subsets, encoding, helper functions, and 5-fold grouped CV for all five
# hurdle components.
# =============================================================================


# Binary integer targets: 1 = extreme (cost > THRESHOLD), 0 = attritional
y_bin_tr <- as.integer(ext_tr)
y_bin_te <- as.integer(ext_te)

# Attritional sub-datasets and encoding
train_att <- train_sev %>% filter(avg_claim_cost <= THRESHOLD)
test_att  <- test_sev  %>% filter(avg_claim_cost <= THRESHOLD)
enc_att   <- encode_predictors(train_att, test_att, target)
X_tr_att  <- enc_att$train;   X_te_att <- enc_att$test
y_tr_att  <- train_att$avg_claim_cost
y_te_att  <- test_att$avg_claim_cost
n_tr_att  <- length(y_tr_att);  n_te_att <- length(y_te_att)

# Extreme sub-datasets and encoding
train_ext <- train_sev %>% filter(avg_claim_cost > THRESHOLD)
test_ext  <- test_sev  %>% filter(avg_claim_cost > THRESHOLD)
enc_ext   <- encode_predictors(train_ext, test_ext, target)
X_tr_ext  <- enc_ext$train;   X_te_ext <- enc_ext$test
y_tr_ext  <- train_ext$avg_claim_cost
y_te_ext  <- test_ext$avg_claim_cost
n_tr_ext  <- length(y_tr_ext);  n_te_ext <- length(y_te_ext)

cat(sprintf(
  "Hurdle subsets:\n  Binary (full claimants): %d train / %d test\n  Attritional: %d train / %d test\n  Extreme    : %d train / %d test\n",
  length(y_bin_tr), length(y_bin_te),
  n_tr_att, n_te_att, n_tr_ext, n_te_ext))

# Binary model metric helper
bin_metrics <- function(y_bin, p, aic, bic)
  c(
    R2        = round(r2_cor(as.numeric(y_bin), p), 4),
    RMSE      = round(rmse(as.numeric(y_bin), p), 4),
    Brier     = round(mean((as.numeric(y_bin) - p)^2, na.rm = TRUE), 4),
    MAE       = round(mae(as.numeric(y_bin), p), 4),
    Binom_Dev = round(binom_dev(as.numeric(y_bin), p), 1),
    AUC       = round(as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(as.integer(y_bin), p,
                                                      quiet = TRUE))), 4),
    Gini      = round(gini_index(as.numeric(y_bin), p), 4),
    AIC       = round(aic, 0),
    BIC       = round(bic, 0)
  )

ll_gamma <- function(y, mu, phi) {
  sum(dgamma(y, shape = 1/phi, scale = mu * phi, log = TRUE))
}

gamma_test_aic_bic <- function(y, pred_mu, phi, k, n) {
  ll <- ll_gamma(y, pred_mu, phi)
  c(AIC = -2 * ll + 2 * k, BIC = -2 * ll + log(n) * k)
}

# =============================================================================
# HURDLE CV: 5-FOLD CLIENT-GROUPED CROSS-VALIDATION
# =============================================================================

cat("\n--- Running Cross-Validation: Hurdle Models ---\n")

cv_hurdle <- lapply(cv_folds$splits, function(sp) {
  tr_all <- analysis(sp)   %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
  va_all <- assessment(sp) %>% filter(n_claims_year > 0)
  if (nrow(tr_all) < 10 || nrow(va_all) < 5) return(NULL)

  yb_tr <- as.integer(tr_all$avg_claim_cost > THRESHOLD)
  yb_va <- as.integer(va_all$avg_claim_cost > THRESHOLD)
  tr_a  <- tr_all %>% filter(avg_claim_cost <= THRESHOLD)
  va_a  <- va_all %>% filter(avg_claim_cost <= THRESHOLD)
  tr_e  <- tr_all %>% filter(avg_claim_cost >  THRESHOLD)
  va_e  <- va_all %>% filter(avg_claim_cost >  THRESHOLD)

  enc_all <- encode_predictors(tr_all, va_all, target)

  # --- Binary logistic ---
  m_bin <- tryCatch(fit_glm(yb_tr, enc_all$train, binomial()), error = function(e) NULL)
  p_bin <- if (!is.null(m_bin)) predict(m_bin, enc_all$test, type = "response") else rep(mean(yb_tr), length(yb_va))

  # --- Attritional models ---
  has_a <- nrow(tr_a) > 10 && nrow(va_a) > 0
  p_ga <- p_la <- pl_a <- yva_a <- numeric(0)
  if (has_a) {
    enc_a <- encode_predictors(tr_a, va_a, target)
    ytr_a <- tr_a$avg_claim_cost;  yva_a <- va_a$avg_claim_cost
    # Gamma (attritional)
    m_ga  <- tryCatch(fit_glm(ytr_a, enc_a$train, Gamma(link = "log")), error = function(e) NULL)
    p_ga  <- if (!is.null(m_ga)) predict(m_ga, enc_a$test, type = "response") else rep(mean(ytr_a), length(yva_a))
    # Log-Normal (attritional)
    m_la  <- tryCatch(fit_lm(log(ytr_a), enc_a$train), error = function(e) NULL)
    sg_a  <- if (!is.null(m_la)) sigma(m_la) else sd(log(ytr_a))
    pl_a  <- if (!is.null(m_la)) predict(m_la, enc_a$test) else rep(mean(log(ytr_a)), length(yva_a))
    p_la  <- ln_backtr(pl_a, sg_a)
  }

    has_e <- nrow(tr_e) > 10 && nrow(va_e) > 0
    p_ge <- p_le <- pl_e <- yva_e <- numeric(0)
    if (has_e) {
      enc_e   <- encode_predictors(tr_e, va_e, target)
      ytr_e   <- tr_e$avg_claim_cost;  yva_e <- va_e$avg_claim_cost

      # Gamma (extreme)
      m_ge  <- tryCatch(fit_glm(ytr_e, enc_e$train, Gamma(link = "log")), error = function(e) NULL)
      p_ge  <- if (!is.null(m_ge)) predict(m_ge, enc_e$test, type = "response") else rep(mean(ytr_e), length(yva_e))

      # Log-Normal (extreme)
      m_le  <- tryCatch(fit_lm(log(ytr_e), enc_e$train), error = function(e) NULL)
      sg_e  <- if (!is.null(m_le)) sigma(m_le) else sd(log(ytr_e))
      pl_e  <- if (!is.null(m_le)) predict(m_le, enc_e$test) else rep(mean(log(ytr_e)), length(yva_e))
      p_le  <- ln_backtr(pl_e, sg_e)
    }

  data.frame(
    # Binary component
    Bin_Brier = mean((as.numeric(yb_va) - p_bin)^2, na.rm = TRUE),
    Bin_RMSE  = rmse(yb_va, p_bin),
    Bin_MAE   = mae(yb_va, p_bin),
    Bin_Dev   = binom_dev(yb_va, p_bin),
    Bin_AUC   = tryCatch(as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(as.integer(yb_va), p_bin,
                                                         quiet = TRUE))),
                         error = function(e) NA_real_),
    Bin_Gini  = tryCatch(gini_index(as.numeric(yb_va), p_bin), error = function(e) NA_real_),
    # Gamma – attritional
    GAtt_R2   = if (has_a) r2_cor(yva_a, p_ga)          else NA_real_,
    GAtt_RMSE = if (has_a) rmse(yva_a, p_ga)            else NA_real_,
    GAtt_MAE  = if (has_a) mae(yva_a, p_ga)             else NA_real_,
    GAtt_Dev  = if (has_a) gamma_dev(yva_a, p_ga)       else NA_real_,
    GAtt_Gini = if (has_a) gini_index(yva_a, p_ga)      else NA_real_,
    # Log-Normal – attritional
    LNAtt_R2   = if (has_a) r2_cor(yva_a, p_la)                                      else NA_real_,
    LNAtt_RMSE = if (has_a) rmse(yva_a, p_la)                                        else NA_real_,
    LNAtt_MAE  = if (has_a) mae(yva_a, p_la)                                         else NA_real_,
    LNAtt_Dev  = if (has_a && length(pl_a) > 0) sum((log(yva_a) - pl_a)^2)          else NA_real_,
    LNAtt_Gini = if (has_a) gini_index(yva_a, p_la)                                  else NA_real_,
    # Gamma – extreme
    GExt_R2   = if (has_e) r2_cor(yva_e, p_ge)          else NA_real_,
    GExt_RMSE = if (has_e) rmse(yva_e, p_ge)            else NA_real_,
    GExt_MAE  = if (has_e) mae(yva_e, p_ge)             else NA_real_,
    GExt_Dev  = if (has_e) gamma_dev(yva_e, p_ge)       else NA_real_,
    GExt_Gini = if (has_e) gini_index(yva_e, p_ge)      else NA_real_,
    # Log-Normal – extreme
    LNExt_R2   = if (has_e) r2_cor(yva_e, p_le)                                      else NA_real_,
    LNExt_RMSE = if (has_e) rmse(yva_e, p_le)                                        else NA_real_,
    LNExt_MAE  = if (has_e) mae(yva_e, p_le)                                         else NA_real_,
    LNExt_Dev  = if (has_e && length(pl_e) > 0) sum((log(yva_e) - pl_e)^2)          else NA_real_,
    LNExt_Gini = if (has_e) gini_index(yva_e, p_le)                                  else NA_real_
  )
})

cv_hurdle_tbl <- dplyr::bind_rows(Filter(Negate(is.null), cv_hurdle))
cv_hurdle_s   <- cv_summary(cv_hurdle_tbl)
cat("Hurdle CV complete.\n")
```


## Binary Component - Logistic Regression (Claims Classification)

The binary classifier determines whether a claimant's average cost exceeds the €900 threshold, partitioning the claimant population into attritional and extreme segments. The model is fitted as a binomial GLM with a logit link on the full claimant training set, using the same predictor matrix as the traditional severity models. A predicted probability above 0.5 would indicate an extreme classification, though the primary use of this model is to produce calibrated probabilities for weighting predictions from the two severity sub-models downstream. Two metrics are introduced that are specific to classification problems. First, Binomial Deviance is defined as $-2\sum[y_i \log(\hat{p}_i) + (1-y_i)\log(1-\hat{p}_i)]$ and directly measures the log-likelihood loss under the binomial assumption — lower is better. Second, AUC (Area Under the ROC Curve) measures the probability that a randomly chosen extreme claimant is assigned a higher predicted probability than a randomly chosen attritional claimant; it ranges from 0.5 (random) to 1.0 (perfect separation) and is independent of the classification threshold. Note that AUC and Gini are monotonically related by Gini = 2 × AUC − 1, so they carry the same rank-ordering information but on different scales. All other metrics (RMSE, MAE) are computed on the probability scale and are consistent with the framework used throughout this document.

```{r hurdle-binary, echo=FALSE}

cat("\n\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70))
cat("\nHURDLE COMPONENT 1: BINARY LOGISTIC REGRESSION\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70), "\n")

# Fit
bin_fit <- fit_glm(y_bin_tr, X_tr_sev, binomial(link = "logit"))

cat("\n--- MODEL SUMMARY ---\n")
print(summary(bin_fit))

# Train
fv_bin <- fitted(bin_fit)
k_bin  <- length(coef(bin_fit))

tr_bin <- bin_metrics(
  y_bin_tr,
  fv_bin,
  AIC(bin_fit),
  BIC(bin_fit)
)

# Test
pred_bin_te <- predict(bin_fit, X_te_sev, type = "response")

ab_te_bin <- logistic_test_aic_bic(
  y_bin_te,
  pred_bin_te,
  k_bin,
  n_te_sev
)

te_bin <- bin_metrics(
  y_bin_te,
  pred_bin_te,
  ab_te_bin["AIC"],
  ab_te_bin["BIC"]
)

# ── Consolidated training & test table ────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- TRAINING & TEST METRICS (Binary Logistic) ---\n")
tbl_bin <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "R2 (Cor2)",
    "RMSE",
    "Brier Score",
    "MAE",
    "Binomial Deviance",
    "AUC",
    "Gini"
  ),

  Binary_Train = unname(tr_bin[
    c("R2","RMSE","Brier","MAE","Binom_Dev","AUC","Gini")
  ]),

  Binary_Test = unname(te_bin[
    c("R2","RMSE","Brier","MAE","Binom_Dev","AUC","Gini")
  ])
)

print(kable(tbl_bin,
            col.names = c("Metric", "Train", "Test"),
            align = c("l","c","c"),
            row.names = FALSE))

# ── CV vs Test comparison ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- CV VS TEST COMPARISON (Binary Logistic) ---\n")
cvte_bin <- data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "RMSE",
    "Brier Score",
    "MAE",
    "Binomial Deviance",
    "AUC",
    "Gini"
  ),

  CV_Mean_SE = c(
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_RMSE"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_RMSE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_Brier"], cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_Brier"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_MAE"],   cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_MAE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_Dev"],   cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_Dev"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_AUC"],   cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_AUC"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["Bin_Gini"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["Bin_Gini"])
  ),

  Test = c(
    te_bin["RMSE"],
    te_bin["Brier"],
    te_bin["MAE"],
    te_bin["Binom_Dev"],
    te_bin["AUC"],
    te_bin["Gini"]
  )
)

print(kable(cvte_bin,
            col.names = c("Metric", "CV Mean (+/-SE)", "Test"),
            align = c("l","c","c"),
            row.names = FALSE))

```

\underline{Discussion:}

The Pearson goodness-of-fit test confirms correct binomial calibration:

- Pearson p = **0.312**, indicating no evidence of miscalibration under the binomial assumption.

The dominant predictors align with structural severity drivers:

- Strong negative effects for older vehicle age bands **(−0.895 for 8–12 years, −0.981 for 12+ years)** indicate that older vehicles are substantially less likely to generate extreme claims, consistent with lower replacement value. 
- Value band and age category remain important contributors.
- The growing_portfolio coefficient of **−1.386** indicates that clients in growing portfolios are considerably less likely to produce extreme claims. 

The vehicle age direction is consistent with the severity models:
- Older vehicles generate lower costs in both the traditional and binary classification settings.

Test performance is consistent with cross-validation:

- Test AUC = **0.6293** (= (1 + Gini) / 2), confirming that the classifier has modest but genuine discriminatory power: it ranks a randomly selected extreme claimant above a randomly selected attritional claimant approximately 63% of the time.
- Test Gini = **0.236**, exceeding CV Gini = **0.191**; this improvement is attributed to **fold-composition differences**, not overfitting.
- The Brier score shows **minimal train–test gap**, confirming stable probability calibration.


## Severity Component for Attritional Claims - Gamma

The Gamma GLM with a log link is fitted exclusively on attritional claimants (avg_claim_cost ≤ €900), representing approximately 91% of the claimant training population. By restricting the training sample to this homogeneous lower-cost segment, the Gamma model is freed from the distributional pull of extreme claims that degrades its fit in the traditional setting. The predictor matrix, encoding pipeline, and cross-validation structure are identical to those used for the traditional models; only the training subset changes. Metrics are reported on the attritional scale, so RMSE, MAE, and deviance values are expected to be substantially lower than their traditional counterparts by construction.

```{r hurdle-gamma-att, echo=FALSE}

cat("\n\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70))
cat("\nHURDLE SEVERITY: GAMMA GLM — ATTRITIONAL CLAIMS (cost <= €900)\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70), "\n")

# Fit
gamma_att_fit  <- fit_glm(y_tr_att, X_tr_att, Gamma(link = "log"))
cat("\n--- MODEL SUMMARY ---\n"); print(summary(gamma_att_fit))

# Training metrics
fv_gamma_att  <- fitted(gamma_att_fit)
phi_gamma_att <- summary(gamma_att_fit)$dispersion
k_gamma_att   <- length(coef(gamma_att_fit)) + 1   # +1 for dispersion parameter
tr_gamma_att  <- sev_metrics(y_tr_att, fv_gamma_att,
                              r2_cor(y_tr_att, fv_gamma_att),
                              AIC(gamma_att_fit), BIC(gamma_att_fit))

# Test metrics
pred_gamma_att_te <- predict(gamma_att_fit, X_te_att, type = "response")
ab_te_g_att       <- gamma_test_aic_bic(y_te_att, pred_gamma_att_te,
                                         phi_gamma_att, k_gamma_att, n_te_att)
te_gamma_att <- sev_metrics(y_te_att, pred_gamma_att_te,
                             r2_cor(y_te_att, pred_gamma_att_te),
                             ab_te_g_att["AIC"], ab_te_g_att["BIC"])

# ── Consolidated training & test table ────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- TRAINING & TEST METRICS (Gamma — Attritional) ---\n")
sev_rows <- c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma_Dev", "LN_Dev [RSS_log]", "Gini")
tbl_g_att <- data.frame(
  Metric      = sev_rows,
  Gamma_Train = unname(tr_gamma_att[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")]),
  Gamma_Test  = unname(te_gamma_att[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")])
)
print(kable(tbl_g_att,
            col.names = c("Metric", "Gamma_Att Train", "Gamma_Att Test"),
            align = c("l","c","c"), row.names = FALSE))

# ── CV vs Test comparison ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- CV VS TEST COMPARISON (Gamma — Attritional) ---\n")
cvte_g_att <- data.frame(
  Metric     = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "Gamma Deviance", "Gini"),
  CV_Mean_SE = c(
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["GAtt_R2"],   cv_hurdle_s$se["GAtt_R2"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["GAtt_RMSE"], cv_hurdle_s$se["GAtt_RMSE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["GAtt_MAE"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["GAtt_MAE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["GAtt_Dev"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["GAtt_Dev"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["GAtt_Gini"], cv_hurdle_s$se["GAtt_Gini"])),
  Test = unname(te_gamma_att[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","Gini")])
)
print(kable(cvte_g_att,
            col.names = c("Metric", "CV Mean (+/-SE)", "Test"),
            align = c("l","c","c"), row.names = FALSE))
```

\underline{Discussion:}

The dispersion result confirms the original Gamma model was heavily misspecified:

- The dispersion parameter φ collapses from **8.13 → 1.073** once extreme claims are removed.
- This shows that the traditional model’s instability was driven almost entirely by **extreme‑claim contamination**, not by structural predictor failure.

Key coefficient shifts highlight the impact of trimming:

- Several sign reversals appear, including the **prior lapse** effect and the **reduced magnitude** of the vehicle age coefficients.
- These reversals indicate that extreme claims were distorting the original parameter estimates.

New predictors emerge once the tail is removed:

- **Vehicle weight** becomes significant.
- **Claim history frequency bands** also enter the model, suggesting more stable behavioural patterns once extreme claims are excluded.

Cross‑validation confirms model stability:

- CV standard error bands are **extremely narrow**, indicating consistent performance across folds and no evidence of fold‑level instability.

## Severity Component for Attritional Claims - Log-Normal

The Log-Normal model is fitted on the natural logarithm of attritional claim costs, exploiting the approximate log-normality of the lower-cost segment after removing the extreme tail. Predictions are returned to the original scale using the bias-corrected back-transformation exp(μ̂ + σ²/2), with σ estimated from the attritional training set. Because the extreme tail is excluded, σ is expected to be smaller than in the traditional Log-Normal model, yielding tighter confidence intervals and better-calibrated back-transformed predictions for the attritional range. The LN_Dev metric (residual sum of squares on the log scale) measures the quantity directly minimised by the OLS fit and provides an interpretable within-segment fit diagnostic.

```{r hurdle-ln-att, echo=FALSE}

cat("\n\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70))
cat("\nHURDLE SEVERITY: LOG-NORMAL — ATTRITIONAL CLAIMS (cost <= €900)\n"); cat(strrep("=", 70), "\n")

# Fit
ln_att_fit   <- fit_lm(log(y_tr_att), X_tr_att)
sigma_ln_att <- sigma(ln_att_fit)
fv_ln_att_log <- fitted(ln_att_fit)
fv_ln_att     <- ln_backtr(fv_ln_att_log, sigma_ln_att)
p_ln_att      <- length(coef(ln_att_fit))
cat("\n--- MODEL SUMMARY ---\n"); print(summary(ln_att_fit))

# Training metrics
ab_tr_ln_att  <- ln_aic_bic(y_tr_att, fv_ln_att_log, sigma_ln_att, p_ln_att, n_tr_att)
tr_ln_att <- sev_metrics(y_tr_att, fv_ln_att,
                          r2_cor(y_tr_att, fv_ln_att),
                          ab_tr_ln_att["AIC"], ab_tr_ln_att["BIC"],
                          pred_log = fv_ln_att_log)

# Test metrics
pred_ln_att_log_te <- predict(ln_att_fit, X_te_att)
pred_ln_att_te     <- ln_backtr(pred_ln_att_log_te, sigma_ln_att)
ab_te_ln_att <- { ll <- ll_lognormal(y_te_att, pred_ln_att_log_te, sigma_ln_att)
                  c(AIC = -2*ll + 2*(p_ln_att+1), BIC = -2*ll + log(n_te_att)*(p_ln_att+1)) }
te_ln_att <- sev_metrics(y_te_att, pred_ln_att_te,
                          r2_cor(y_te_att, pred_ln_att_te),
                          ab_te_ln_att["AIC"], ab_te_ln_att["BIC"],
                          pred_log = pred_ln_att_log_te)

# ── Consolidated training & test table ────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- TRAINING & TEST METRICS (Log-Normal — Attritional) ---\n")
tbl_ln_att <- data.frame(
  Metric    = sev_rows,
  LN_Train  = unname(tr_ln_att[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")]),
  LN_Test   = unname(te_ln_att[c("R2","RMSE","MAE","Gamma_Dev","LN_Dev","Gini")])
)
print(kable(tbl_ln_att,
            col.names = c("Metric", "LN_Att Train", "LN_Att Test"),
            align = c("l","c","c"), row.names = FALSE))

# ── CV vs Test comparison ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cat("\n--- CV VS TEST COMPARISON (Log-Normal — Attritional) ---\n")
cvte_ln_att <- data.frame(
  Metric     = c("R2 (Cor2)", "RMSE", "MAE", "LN Deviance [RSS_log]", "Gini"),
  CV_Mean_SE = c(
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["LNAtt_R2"],   cv_hurdle_s$se["LNAtt_R2"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["LNAtt_RMSE"], cv_hurdle_s$se["LNAtt_RMSE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["LNAtt_MAE"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["LNAtt_MAE"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["LNAtt_Dev"],  cv_hurdle_s$se["LNAtt_Dev"]),
    fmt_cv(cv_hurdle_s$mean["LNAtt_Gini"], cv_hurdle_s$se["LNAtt_Gini"])),
```
